name: The Mycelium Bridge
image_prompt: Interior of a living starship bridge. Organic walls and ceiling sculpted from bone-white fungal wood, thick vein-like tendrils glowing teal and cyan blue-green bioluminescence, veins branching across every surface like nerves. Lozenge-shaped chamber, arched organic ceiling, soft mycelium floor in pale ivory. Forward wall shows a large dark transparent hull patch with starfield and deep space visible through it. A bowl-shaped pilot's seat-hollow grown into the forward floor. A ring of low curved benches of the same fungal material in the center. No straight edges, no metal, no industrial panels, no consoles. Warm moist rainforest-at-dusk atmosphere, faint glowing mist. Colors: ivory cream, bone white, teal, cyan, black starfield. Painterly organic biological sci-fi, alien bioship aesthetic.
location: Command deck of the ISV Cerebellum, a third-generation living starship in slow transit between two unremarkable stars. The hull is a symbiotic myceliate lattice grown on a diamond scaffold, meaning the ship is part crystal and part fungus and will not let anyone forget that it is alive. The bridge is a lozenge-shaped chamber about thirty feet long, ceiling arched six feet at the crown, walls of warm bone-white fungal wood veined with glowing blue-green biolight tracks that pulse in time with something nobody has explained to the crew. The floor is a single slab of mycelium grown over a copper heat exchanger. It is soft underfoot. It is warm.
time: Ship clock reads 34:12 of a 40-hour day the crew designed for themselves three weeks in. Outside the bridge, the ship keeps its own sense of time that does not map to any of the standard reckonings. Ask the ship what time it is and a status ring somewhere on the wall turns violet for a second, then returns to blue, and the question is not answered.
weather: Bridge climate is tuned to a specific rainforest floor at dusk. 22 degrees, 78 percent humidity. A fine mist issues from ceiling pores every eleven minutes and is absorbed back into the wall biology within a few seconds of touching it. Nothing ever gets wet. The air moves in slow convection currents that the crew has learned to read. When the ship is worried about something, the air gets still.

atmosphere.lighting: The biolight veins throw a low blue-green glow over every surface. Brighter near the helm, dimmer at the rear. When anyone raises their voice the veins nearest them pulse brighter by about ten percent, a feedback mechanism the ship developed on its own and nobody has figured out how to turn off. Overhead, a translucent patch of hull shows the darkness outside and the occasional track of a distant star crossing slowly. Faces in this light look underwater.
atmosphere.sound: A constant low wet breathing from the walls, as of a sleeping animal. The ship respires on a 90-second cycle. Footsteps on the mycelium floor make almost no sound. Voices carry far but are softened. A faint pop every few minutes from somewhere deep in the hull as a growth node matures. The helm station makes no sound of its own; it responds by warming under the hand.
atmosphere.crowd: The bridge was grown to hold seven stations but five is comfortable, and two is intimate in a way people do not always expect. The ship does not distinguish between someone standing in the bridge and someone sitting in the bridge; the walls treat both as presence. Nobody pushes past anyone here. The floor nudges you around each other.
atmosphere.smell: Warm bread, cut cedar, damp clean earth after rain. Something faintly sweet behind it all, like overripe fruit, that the crew no longer consciously notices but visitors always comment on. When someone is tense, the sweet note intensifies until they relax.

layout.helm_cup: A bowl-shaped indentation at the forward end of the bridge where the pilot sits cross-legged on the mycelium floor, hand resting in a palm-shaped hollow in the wall. The helm is grown, not built; the ship steers by reading pressure and temperature from whichever pilot is currently in the cup. The cup is not labeled. A small cushion has been left in it by someone who prefers to sit on cotton rather than fungus.
layout.council_ring: Seven low benches grown in a circle near the center of the bridge, each the exact height of the person who last sat on it for more than ten minutes. The benches remember. Sitting at the ring brings every voice in the circle closer by a fraction of a decibel. Standing outside the ring makes you quieter to everyone inside it. This has caused arguments.
layout.signal_alcove: A recessed nook on the starboard wall where communications with the home fleet, such as they are, pass through a bioelectric interface that translates radio into pulses of light along the biolight veins. Listening here is more like reading than hearing. The last transmission is eight days overdue. The alcove wall has started to look a little gray around the interface port. Nobody has commented.
layout.fungal_archive: A broad shelf recessed into the aft wall where the ship grows its own memory. Each decision the crew has made is encoded as a small ridged node in a row along the shelf, like a coral with the most recent branches on top. Running a finger along the nodes lets the ship replay the decision context in faint sounds and smells; most of the crew does this unconsciously when they pass.
layout.observation_lung: A small chamber off the port side where the hull is thinnest and the biolight ceases entirely. Inside, an oval patch of the hull has been grown nearly transparent, and you can stand with your forehead against it and feel the ship breathe while staring at the dark. The only place on the bridge where you can hear your own heartbeat clearly. Most people do not stay long.

objects_of_note: A small ceramic cup on the edge of the helm cup, half-full of tea that has been half-full for three days. The ship refuses to absorb it. Nobody has asked why.

A hand-drawn map pinned to the wall between the signal alcove and the council ring showing the Cerebellum's path through the last seven systems, annotated in three handwritings. A fourth handwriting has added question marks next to the last two entries.
A ship's logbook left open on one of the council ring benches, last entry reading 'she is dreaming again.' The capitalized 'she' has been corrected to lowercase and corrected back again.
A spore vial sealed inside a small glass case set into the fungal archive wall. The label reads 'parent, gen 2, do not open.' The vial glows faintly at night when the bridge is empty.

A coil of copper wire on the floor of the observation lung, bent into no particular shape. When the ship is calm, the wire is cold. When the ship is agitated, it is warm enough to notice. Nobody on the current crew brought it aboard.
mood: Domestic and uncanny. The bridge is a living thing that has chosen to host the crew, and the crew is slowly beginning to realize that the relationship runs both directions. Decisions made here reverberate through the hull for days. Frameworks brought in here have to contend with the fact that the room is listening, the room has preferences, and the room has a long memory. The ship does not argue. It just breathes, and the air smells a little sweeter or a little greener depending on what the crew is about to do. Everyone on this bridge is a guest of something older than they are.
